Retrofit-First Public-Interest Feasibility Tool

A data center must prove it belongs.

Project Aether is a feasibility framework for responsible digital infrastructure: reduce unnecessary compute, reuse existing powered sites, prove site fit, minimize water and grid burden, and make every remaining impact measurable. It treats cooling as one part of a larger civic system: power, water, land, heat, hardware, zoning, public trust, and long-term accountability.

Start evidence review Open cost model Screen site fit

Documented siting pathways

Five federal candidate-site pathways are available for evidence review

This is an initial federal evidence set, not a comprehensive U.S. ranking. Each site remains subject to Aether's hard gates and professional verification.

Why This Matters

Data centers are becoming part of the physical landscape of everyday life. They compete for power, water, land, labor, public money, and political trust.

What Aether Asks

The project asks whether a facility earns its footprint before it earns its renderings: first through demand reduction and reuse, then through proved site fit and public accountability.

How to read inputs, assumptions, results, and evidence
User inputDefault assumptionCalculated resultVerified evidence

Screening values remain assumptions until a named source or reviewed artifact is attached.

Project Aether Doctrine

The Hierarchy Before the Hardware

Aether starts from a simple standard: the best facility is not the one with the most elegant cooling system. It is the one that creates the least unnecessary heat, proves it belongs on its site, protects public resources, and makes its remaining impacts measurable.

Reduce demand. Reuse infrastructure. Prove site fit. Optimize what remains. Verify every claimed benefit.
1. Reduce computeUse useful work per kWh, server utilization, idle-power reduction, workload placement, and hardware efficiency as first-order design variables.
2. Reuse infrastructureFavor existing powered shells, data-center retrofits, brownfields, and district-energy adjacencies before new construction.
3. Prove site fitRequire grid capacity, water resilience, zoning path, flood/ecology clearance, and community-benefit terms before land logic becomes design logic.
4. Cool the remainderApply liquid cooling, airflow containment, thermal storage, passive shell design, and controls to the load that remains after efficiency work.
5. Reuse heat honestlyCount heat reuse only when route, temperature, seasonality, metering, heat-pump design, and buyer commitment are real.
6. Report publiclyPublish PUE, WUE, CUE, generator runtime, water samples, heat export, outages, complaints, and expansion-readiness metrics.
Aether Verdict

One recommendation, auditable rationale

The dashboard separates eligibility, evidence completeness, and recommendation confidence before presenting an executive path: retrofit, redesign, new build, demand reduction, or no-build. Optimization cannot compensate for a failed hard gate.

Role-specific reading guide

Public reading guide

Prioritize grid burden, water risk, generator runtime, heat reuse, noise, tax incentives, community benefit, expansion limits, and public reporting.

Aether first principle

The project must pass public-resource gates before design advances

Aether is a whole-system decision process: reduce the compute load, reuse an existing site if possible, prove the grid and water case, secure a real heat customer, and give the public enforceable benefits before a new build is treated as reasonable.

1. Reduce demand firstScore useful work per kWh, server utilization, idle-power waste, workload placement, carbon-aware scheduling, and hardware efficiency before adding cooling equipment.
2. Retrofit before new buildCompare existing data centers, powered shells, brownfields, warehouses, and district-heating adjacencies. A new building must beat these alternatives.
3. Prove power, water, and zoningRequire utility load evidence, queue/interconnection status, transformer and switchgear plan, local water/drought plan, and a real entitlement path.
4. Identify nonviable sites earlyHigh water stress, ratepayer cost-shift risk, weak community value, environmental justice burden, missing heat offtake, or hostile zoning can override an efficient design.

Nonviability conditions

  • Power is unavailable within the project schedule or ordinary ratepayers carry dedicated upgrade costs.
  • Water use depends on a stressed watershed, weak drought plan, or uncertain discharge permissions.
  • Zoning requires a fragile entitlement path without enforceable public conditions.
  • Heat reuse lacks a buyer, pipe route, temperature plan, seasonal load, and metering.
  • Environmental justice burden, noise, traffic, or generator emissions outweigh local value.

Feasibility standards

  • Demand reduction and retrofit alternatives are documented before new-build design begins.
  • Grid, water, zoning, fire, insurance, and commissioning evidence is collected early.
  • Community benefits are binding permit conditions, not branding promises.
  • Expansion is conditional on measured performance from earlier phases.
  • Public dashboards make performance visible after approval.
Aether Score Explained

Threshold requirements are distinct from optimization levers

The score separates conditions that can stop a project from features that improve a project. A better facade, lower PUE, or smarter AI control layer cannot compensate for failed water, grid, zoning, life-safety, or environmental justice gates.

Threshold Requirements

Grid fitAvailable MW, interconnection path, substation upgrades, transformer procurement, and ratepayer protection.Gate
Water fitWater source, WUE, drought mode, discharge, basin stress, and public sampling.Gate
Zoning pathUse approvals, special permits, setbacks, generator rules, noise, traffic, and appeal risk.Gate
Public burdenEnvironmental justice, cumulative impacts, community benefits, expansion limits, and complaint response.Gate

Optimization levers

Compute efficiencyUseful work per kWh, server utilization, idle-power reduction, and workload timing.Optimize
Cooling systemLiquid cooling, airflow, thermal storage, heat rejection, and commissioning quality.Optimize
Heat reuseThermal export quality, buyer commitment, seasonal match, and metered public benefit.Conditional
Supervisory controlsFault detection, setpoint optimization, demand response, and workload coordination inside hard safety limits.Optimize
Retrofit and Reuse Playbook

New construction is only one possible pathway

Aether treats reuse as a serious design path, not a compromise. Existing infrastructure can reduce land disturbance, entitlement risk, embodied carbon, and schedule uncertainty when grid, safety, and cooling upgrades are feasible.

Powered warehouse conversion

Useful when power and fiber are close but the building needs major MEP transformation.

  • Inspect slab, roof, clear height, fire access, drainage, and electrical rooms.
  • Model acoustic, generator, and heat-rejection impacts.
  • Reject if structural or utility upgrades erase reuse benefits.

Existing data-center modernization

Often the cleanest path when the shell, utility service, and operating permits already exist.

  • Upgrade containment, controls, liquid-cooling readiness, UPS, and monitoring.
  • Measure useful work per kWh before buying new equipment.
  • Use commissioning and fault detection as primary savings tools.

Brownfield industrial reuse

Can align remediation, tax-base repair, and infrastructure reuse.

  • Require Phase I/II environmental work, vapor risk, soil/groundwater sampling, and closure plan.
  • Confirm title, access, easements, flood risk, and community acceptance.
  • Do not allow remediation value to obscure grid or water burden.

District-heating co-location

Most credible when a thermal customer is near, large, and year-round.

  • Prove pipe route, temperature, heat pump, seasonal demand, metering, and backup heat.
  • Separate decarbonization value from financial payback.
  • Count heat revenue only after a term sheet or offtake exists.

Substation-adjacent reuse

Can shorten the power path but raises public scrutiny.

  • Verify queue position, transformer lead time, upgrade cost allocation, and ratepayer protection.
  • Screen noise, visual impact, transmission easements, and emergency access.
  • Expansion should require new performance proof.

No-building efficiency retrofit

The most overlooked option is reducing demand without adding square footage.

  • Increase utilization, retire idle gear, shift flexible workloads, and improve model efficiency.
  • Pair software optimization with power and thermal telemetry.
  • Report avoided MWh as a project outcome.

Interactive conceptual thermal model with separate public and engineering views. Visible ducts and pipes show airflow, coolant supply and return, heat reuse, heat rejection, and electrical supply. Equivalent values appear in adjacent text.

Component
Simple view follows heat from IT work through capture, reuse, and rejection. Line width reflects modeled thermal share; motion shows direction only. Yellow dashed lines carry electricity.
How to read this

Follow the heat, not the floor plan

Every megawatt used by the IT equipment becomes heat. The diagram shows where that heat is captured, whether it can be reused, and how the remainder leaves the facility.

Cool-air supplyWarm-air returnCoolant supplyWarm coolant returnHeat export and returnElectrical supply
Selected componentIT workload and racks

The current workload produces the thermal load that every downstream system must manage.

Conceptual screening model—not CFD, a control sequence, or stamped mechanical design. Temperatures and flow splits are explanatory proxies until replaced with equipment schedules and site weather data.

Where the modeled IT heat goes

Illustrative allocation of current IT heat after heat reuse is counted first; the remaining load is split between free/passive and mechanically assisted rejection.

--Useful heat exported
--Free or passive rejection
--Mechanically assisted rejection

Engineering-view readings

Screening proxies that make supply, return, and rejection paths explicit.

Supply air--Modeled rack inlet
Warm-air return--Illustrative hot-aisle temperature
Coolant supply--Illustrative facility-water supply
Coolant return--Modeled warm return to heat exchanger
Heat export--Supply temperature after lift when needed
Heat rejected--IT heat not exported to a customer

Pessimistic economics

Cost, Payback, and Missing-Cost Stress Test

The estimator treats heat reuse, incentives, solar/BIPV, and AI optimization as upside only after they are contracted, verified, or measured. It includes land, due diligence, utility deposits, interconnection studies, long-lead transformers and switchgear, insurance, cybersecurity, commissioning, maintenance contracts, downtime risk, owner costs, and contingency.

Cost assumptions

User-adjustable inputDefault loaded

These are conceptual ranges. Real numbers come from utility studies, design development, bids, land contracts, and permit conditions.

Includes setbacks, substation, stormwater, security, future expansion, acoustic buffer, and potential heat user / greenhouse pad.

Higher rigor adds monitoring wells, more parameters, more years of post-construction sampling, and public dashboarding.

Funds workforce training, heat discount, public reporting, landscaping, traffic mitigation, and local resilience upgrades.

Zoning + Environment

Location Optimizer

The tool does not identify a purchasable parcel. It converts the shared national location selection into a screening profile, compares nearby and statewide alternatives, and shows the parcel-level checks that must be proven: zoning district, special-use path, grid interconnection, water availability, floodplain, wetlands, endangered species, noise, generators, traffic, taxes, and local benefit.

Location alternatives

The current place and a representative set of same-county and statewide alternatives are screened with the same project assumptions. Selecting one updates every location-sensitive model.

National directory connectedChoose any listed state, county equivalent, city, town, or Census place from the shared selector above.

Real-World Siting Evidence

Initial Federal Candidate-Site Evidence Set

Five documented federal pathways are shown as an initial evidence set—not a comprehensive U.S. ranking or an Aether endorsement. Each entry appears because a U.S. Department of Energy office documented a site-specific AI data-center pathway through a lease, solicitation, or negotiation selection.

Five public federal development pathways, separated from Aether screening proxies

The evidence cards use official federal announcements only. Aether then applies the current project size, cooling choice, and power strategy as a separate screening layer. The model screen is not presented as real-world approval.

Inclusion thresholdA site must have a named federal owner, a public site-specific lease, solicitation, or negotiation selection, and an official source that identifies the development pathway. Commercial marketing claims and unverified “shovel-ready” listings are excluded.
Important: Federal selection or solicitation does not prove utility capacity, water availability, environmental clearance, community acceptance, project finance, or final lease approval. Each candidate must still pass Aether’s hard gates and all applicable federal, state, tribal, and local reviews. Source status and site geography reviewed July 21, 2026.

Water and Ecology

Testing Before and After Construction

Water must be measured before the facility changes the site, during construction when sediment and spills are most likely, and after operation begins. The model treats water monitoring as a public accountability system, not a one-time report.

Monitoring network diagram

This visualization is summarized in the adjacent text and numeric results.

Water monitoring concept showing baseline, construction, operations, and closure sampling points.

Sampling plan

Complete water accounting

Withdrawal, consumption, treatment, and discharge balance

Water withdrawal is not treated as equivalent to net consumption. The three operating modes expose how heat waves and drought restrictions alter the balance.

Screening basis
Operating modeTotal withdrawalReclaimed supplyPotable / fresh supplyEvaporationDriftBlowdownTreatment lossDischargeNet consumption

Monitoring media and direction

MediumDirection / stationBaselineConstructionOperationsIncident / closure

Interpretation

Aether Impact Suite

Retrofit-First Feasibility, Public Benefit, and Evidence Discipline

The simulator functions as an evidence-based public-interest decision tool. It can recommend demand reduction, retrofit, brownfield reuse, new build, or no-build. Missing proof items actively penalize the new-build case: grid proof, water and drought plan, zoning path, heat offtake, brownfield comparison, and enforceable community benefit.

Impact controls

User-adjustable inputDefault loaded

These controls coordinate the thermal, cost, water, and site-selection engine. They do not replace stamped engineering or local legal review.

When enabled, major claims must be backed by hourly data, public monitoring, contracts, and lifecycle accounting.

Core proof packageDocumentary evidence status

Incomplete items reduce eligibility. Check an item only when documentary evidence has been reviewed.

Calculated resultEmbodied carbon--Construction + Major Hardware Proxy
Calculated resultCommunity ledger--Benefits Minus Burdens

0. Core proof package Incomplete

Aether applies a strict standard: architectural quality cannot compensate for missing interconnection proof, an insufficient water plan, no heat buyer, unresolved zoning, or unenforceable community benefits.

Aether Supervisory Optimizer

AI recommends and optimizes; safety systems remain authoritative

The control layer is a bounded supervisory optimizer above BMS/EPMS/DCIM, not an autonomous operator. It watches racks, cooling, weather, grid, water, heat customers, and workloads, then recommends or dispatches low-risk setpoints inside hard limits enforced by BMS, PLCs, fire/life-safety systems, electrical protection, and human procedures.

AI control assumptions

User-adjustable inputDefault loaded

These controls estimate the incremental supervisory layer above ordinary BMS/EPMS/DCIM. Safety interlocks, electrical protection, fire systems, emergency shutdowns, and human override remain outside the optimizer.

Calculated resultAI CAPEX--Incremental Controls, Sensors, Software, Integration
Calculated resultAnnual run cost--Software, MLOps, Cybersecurity, Operators
Calculated resultCooling energy cut--Against Static Control Sequence
Calculated resultTotal energy cut--Whole-Facility Incremental Estimate
Calculated resultPUE improvement--Estimated Reduction in Overhead
Calculated resultSimple payback--After Annual Run Cost

Aether Supervisory Optimizer architecture

The AI does not replace safety systems. It proposes or dispatches setpoints inside hard guardrails: rack inlet limits, humidity/dew point, fluid pressure, pump minimums, valve travel limits, battery state of charge, fire/life-safety overrides, water discharge limits, and utility curtailment rules.

What it does, step by step

The strongest value is not generic automation claims; it is continuous coordination: less overcooling, fewer hidden faults, better pump/fan speed control, pre-cooling only when useful, workload timing, thermal-storage dispatch, and proof that heat reuse is real.

Why it makes the building more efficient

Estimated Performance Contribution

The tool caps the cooling-energy improvement below the publicly reported 40% class cooling result unless the design has high sensors, high authority, and an advanced model. Whole-facility savings are smaller because IT load still dominates.

Implementation cost breakdown

Cost bucketConceptual estimateWhat is includedCost trigger

Deployment path

Safety, governance, and failure modes

Authority and fallback

AI action authority, confidence, and safe fallback

Every proposed action is assigned an explicit authority state. Fire/life-safety, electrical protection, and emergency shutdown remain outside AI authority.

Authority loading
Control actionCurrent authorityConfidenceGuardrailFallback state
Delivery realism

Operational prerequisites define the project

Aether treats financing, permitting, procurement, operator readiness, stakeholder authority, and expansion governance as core design systems. These requirements determine whether the concept can withstand review by utilities, regulators, insurers, lenders, operators, community representatives, and public officials.

Financing realism

  • Identify who pays for grid upgrades, substation work, studies, deposits, and delay risk.
  • Run the project without heat revenue, incentives, or AI savings as the lender base case.
  • Track tax abatements, insurance, cyber controls, spare parts, replacement cycles, and downtime exposure.
  • Require a sensitivity table for power price, schedule delay, equipment lead time, and utilization.

Permitting timeline

0-6 moSite option, utility inquiry, zoning counsel, ESA, ALTA, wetlands, water baseline, public-risk screen.
6-18 moInterconnection studies, entitlement path, environmental permits, traffic/noise, fire review, design development.
18-36 moLong-lead procurement, substation/switchgear, civil work, shell, MEP, controls, commissioning, public dashboard setup.

Stakeholder map

  • Utility and grid operator: power, interconnection, curtailment, cost allocation.
  • Planning board and zoning staff: land use, conditions, hearings, appeals.
  • Water authority and Environmental regulators: withdrawals, discharge, sampling, drought mode.
  • Fire marshal, insurer, lender, heat offtaker, neighbors, operator, and cloud customer: operating reality.

Operator readiness

  • 24/7 staffing model, escalation tree, spare-parts plan, maintenance contracts, and training.
  • BMS/EPMS/DCIM historian, cybersecurity segmentation, manual fallback, and recovery drills.
  • Commissioning plan, seasonal tuning, leak response, outage drills, and model-validation cadence.

Expansion governance

  • No expansion by default; each phase earns more MW through measured performance.
  • Release conditions: PUE/WUE/CUE targets, generator limits, water samples, heat-export proof, complaint response.
  • Require public re-review if load, water use, generator runtime, or substation footprint changes materially.

Operational prerequisites

  • Utility studies, transformer delivery, fire marshal review, insurance acceptance, and stormwater permits.
  • Noise modeling, backup-generator emissions, easements, title, cybersecurity, commissioning, and operator training.
  • Public complaint response, maintenance contracts, decommissioning funding, and closure sampling.
Delivery and downside ownership

Policy and government affairs

Public policy framework for responsible data-center siting

This section organizes neutral legislative, regulatory, zoning, and administrative options by level of government. It is designed for public officials, planning staff, utilities, community representatives, and applicants who need clear standards without assuming that every site should be approved or rejected.

Jurisdiction filter

User-adjustable inputDefault loaded

Use state, county, city, and ZIP as an intake path. ZIP codes are useful for routing a viewer to likely jurisdictions, but they are not sufficient on their own because many ZIP codes cross municipal or county boundaries.

Includes counties, parishes, boroughs, independent cities, municipios, districts, and other county equivalents available in the directory.

Searches the embedded directory without sending the jurisdiction to an external service. Census-designated places are included for geographic intake but may not have municipal authority.

ZIP codes are mailing geographies, not legal jurisdictions; verify parcel, utility, watershed, county, and municipal boundaries separately.

Embedded national directory56 state, district, and territory choices · 3,229 counties or equivalents · 48,515 place/county records.
Recommended structure: use ZIP code as a convenience lookup, then resolve the actual city, county, utility territory, watershed, air district, school district, and state commission jurisdiction before showing local requirements.
Calculated resultPrimary objectiveProof before scaleLarge digital loads should demonstrate grid, water, zoning, safety, and community fit before expansion.
Embedded evidence directoryJurisdiction coverage56 jurisdictions3,229 counties or equivalents and 48,515 place/county records are searchable after a state or territory is selected.
Calculated routingSelected jurisdictionNational viewSelect a state, county equivalent, and place to generate jurisdiction-labeled screening templates.
Calculated resultEquity guardrailRatepayer and water protectionInfrastructure costs and scarce water capacity should be explicitly allocated and disclosed.
LegislationStatutes, charter amendments, moratoria with study periods, tax-credit conditions, procurement requirements, and public reporting laws.
RegulationUtility commission rules, water-withdrawal permits, air permits, building/fire code interpretations, and environmental review procedures.
Zoning and agreementsUse tables, special-use permits, performance standards, development agreements, community-benefit agreements, and expansion triggers.
Coverage and use: The embedded place directory is derived from U.S. Census Bureau geography and includes incorporated municipalities plus Census-designated places. Place records are from US City List under CC BY 4.0; official boundary and naming checks should use the current U.S. Census Gazetteer Files. Directory inclusion is not evidence that a place has zoning authority or a data-center-specific law.
LevelJurisdictionPolicy instrumentPurposeDraft proposal languageAdministrative owner

Generated policy concepts; local legal review is required.

Policy provenance

Authority, citation, effective date, and verification status

Generated proposals are model-policy templates unless a current authority and citation have been independently verified. Geographic routing does not establish legal applicability.

Permanent evidence labels
Policy itemEvidence labelIssuing bodyEffective dateCitation statusLast verification
Public data dashboard

Accountability after approval

The project remains accountable after construction. Public reporting turns promises into measurable obligations and creates a basis for expansion limits, community-benefit enforcement, and operational improvement.

Calculated resultPUE--Facility power divided by IT power; reported quarterly and during heat events.
Calculated resultWUE--Cooling water intensity with drought-mode notes and basin context.
Calculated resultCUE--Carbon intensity using hourly grid conditions where available.
Calculated resultGenerator runtime<25 hAnnual testing and emergency runtime, with emissions conditions.
Calculated resultHeat exported--Metered thermal MWh delivered to a contracted customer.
Calculated resultWater samplesQuarterlyGroundwater, surface water, outfall, discharge temperature, and chemistry.
Calculated resultComplaints30-day closeoutNoise, traffic, lighting, odor, and water concerns tracked publicly.
Calculated resultCBA spendingAuditedWorkforce, resilience, heat discount, public reporting, and local mitigation funds.
Non-compensatory public score

Eligibility, comparative suitability, and optimization

A favorable composite is withheld whenever a hard gate is failed or critical evidence is missing. Suitability only compares eligible alternatives; optimization applies after selection.

Composite withheld
1 · Eligibility gates--

2 · Comparative suitability--

3 · Post-selection optimization--

Model Scope and Research Concepts

Credible because its boundaries are explicit

Aether is a decision-support model, not a certification. It identifies which assumptions deserve engineering, legal, financial, Environmental, and community review before a project claims feasibility.

Known Limits of the Model

  • Does not replace utility interconnection studies, environmental permitting, or zoning counsel.
  • Does not replace CFD, MEP/electrical design, fire/life-safety review, or commissioning.
  • Does not prove project finance, legal entitlement, insurance acceptance, or public consent.
  • Does not certify heat revenue, incentives, clean-energy matching, or expansion rights.

Research Concepts: Educational and Non-Bankable

  • Micro-wind in exhaust paths: visual learning tool, not an energy strategy.
  • Piezoelectric floors: engagement feature, not material power recovery.
  • Chip thermoelectrics: research topic, not a business-case lever.
  • Solar skin/BIPV: shading, storytelling, and partial generation; not 24/7 facility power.
Individual and Institutional Engagement

Practical Engagement Pathways

Individuals, institutions, and local stakeholders can create demand-side pressure, ask more rigorous permitting questions, and normalize higher standards for digital infrastructure.

Build a civic checklist

User-adjustable inputDefault loaded

Generated checklist based on the selected role and local status.

Role-specific evidence questions

Connect each public question to the model output it tests

The generated checklist changes by role and points users toward the exact Aether section or evidence artifact relevant to the question.

Traceable engagement
Reference anchors

Sources and assumptions

These links are embedded so the HTML can stand alone during a presentation. They are not a substitute for local counsel, a licensed civil/Environmental engineer, or utility interconnection studies.

Project Aether v6.9 is a conceptual, national location-aware decision-support model. It is not a construction specification, zoning opinion, environmental permit application, financial offer, or engineering stamp.
